rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BESSIE L. FRANKLIN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Protective Order. (Added For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: As to Movant's second argument, that Respondent's discovery requests are irrelevant, the Court disagrees. The standard for determining whether discovery is relevant is set forth in Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2017.010, which states in relevant part, "any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Here, Respondent is seeking financial records and correspondence concerning Michelle Franklin's misappropriation of funds because the resolution of Ms. Franklin's misappropriation of funds will have an impact on Respondent's distributive share of Decedent's estate. Respondent has objected to the Stipulation and agreement that Administrator has entered into with Ms. Franklin concerning her misappropriation of funds, asserting that the offset against Ms. Franklin's distributive share should also include statutory interest. The financial records and correspondence Respondent seeks to discover are relevant to establishing the amount of funds misappropriated by Ms. Franklin, and the reduction of Ms. Franklin's distributive share as a consequence, to which Respondent is objecting. Therefore, Respondent's discovery requests are relevant. As to Movant's third argument, that Respondent's discovery requests are unwarranted, oppressive and unduly burdensome, this Court disagrees. A party seeking a protective order must show good cause for issuance of the order by a preponderance of evidence. (Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2031.060; Stadish v. Superior Court (1999) 71 Cal.App.4th 1130, 1145.). Movant asserts that discovery is unwarranted because half of the documents Respondent seeks can be obtained from Michelle Franklin directly, rather than from Movant which would incur an expense to the Estate. (Movant Memorandum of Points and Authorities [hereafter "MPA"] at p. 5:1-5). While this may be factually accurate, Movant has not established by a preponderance of the evidence that Respondent's discovery requests are unwarranted such that a protective order is necessary, simply because Respondent has alternative methods available from which to acquire the financial records and correspondence she seeks. Movant further argues Respondent's discovery requests are oppressive because they create a burden on Movant to respond to discovery and file the instant Motion. (MPA at p. 5:6). As set forth in W. Pico Furniture Co. v. Superior Court (1961), 56 Cal.2d 407, 417-18, "to support an objection of oppression there must be some showing either of an intent to create an unreasonable burden or that the ultimate effect of the burden is incommensurate with the relief sought." Although Movant has asserted Respondent is seeking discovery based on her personal animus toward Michelle Franklin, Movant has not established by preponderance of the evidence that Respondent's intent is to create an unreasonable burden or that the effect of the burden is incommensurate with the relief sought. Indeed, it appears that producing some correspondence and bank records presents a very modest burden on the Administrator, not one that is oppressive. (part 2 of 3)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BESSIE L. FRANKLIN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion For Protective Order. (Added For Purposes Of Posting The Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: Administrator Keith Franklin shall serve responses to Petitioner's Demand for Identification, Production, and Inspection of Documents and Other Tangible Things to Administrator Keith Franklin (Set One), nos. 1 and 2 within 15 days of service of a Notice of Entry of Order. Administrator's request for sanctions is DENIED. Respondent's request for a surcharge of the Administrator's share of the Estate and/or attorney's statutory fees by $2,700, is DENIED. The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. (part 3 of 3)

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF BESSIE L. FRANKLIN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Protective Order; Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Administrator Keith Franklin (hereafter "Administrator" or "Movant")'s Motion for a Protective Order; Sanctions. Administrator seeks an Order quashing the discovery requests propounded on him by beneficiary Respondent Nejra Allen on or about June 3, 2024, on the grounds that Ms. Allen lacks standing to propound discovery, the discovery sought is irrelevant, and is unwarranted, unduly burdensome and oppressive. Movant also seeks sanctions against Respondent of at least $6,000.00 for misuse of the discovery process. Administrator's Motion for a Protective order is DENIED. As a preliminary matter, Respondent has standing to propound discovery. Parties to a probate proceeding are entitled to conduct discovery. (Mota v. Superior Court (2007), 156 Cal.App.4th 351, 355 (hereafter "Mota").) In Mota, the court held that Ms. Mota, a trust beneficiary, was entitled to conduct discovery related to her objection to the trustee's petition to approve his accounting and for final distribution. (Id. at p.353) (See also Forthmann v. Boyer (2002), 97 Cal.App.4th 977, 986, holding that a beneficiary would have had the right to conduct discovery if he had filed an objection to the trustee's accounting petition, because "[i]n the absence of specific objections to such report there is no way to determine what discovery, if any, was 'relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action.' [citing Code of Civ. Proc., sec. 2017(a), repealed in 2004 to facilitate non-substantive reorganization of the rules governing civil discovery].) Similarly, in Coberly v. Superior Court (1965), 231 Cal.App.2d 685, 690 (hereafter "Coberly"), a trust beneficiary objected to a professional trustee's petition to approve its accounting. The Court in Coberly held that probate matters fell within the category of "special proceedings" defined by the Discovery Act to constitute an action, and that the discovery procedures are generally available for use in probate accountings. Here, the Administrator filed a "Stipulation of Michelle Franklin for Reduction of Share" on April 11, 2024. This document revealed that Michelle Franklin had improperly transferred over $107,000 from decedent's account at the Police Credit Union to her own account, and then apparently spent that money. The stipulation indicated that the Administrator and Michelle Franklin agreed that $107,262.62 would be deducted "from her share of the Estate" to reflect the sum she misappropriated "in order to avoid unnecessary litigation concerning the Estate." Respondent filed an Objection to the Stipulation on April 17, 2024. Therefore, consistent with Mota and Coberly, Respondent has standing to propound discovery because she has filed an Objection to the Stipulation filed by Administrator. Furthermore, assuming arguendo that Respondent's Objection to the Stipulation does not qualify as a "probate proceeding," as defined by Mota, because there is no pending action, Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2035.010 permits discovery even where an action is not yet pending. Pursuant to Cal. Code of Civil Proc. sec. 2035.010, "[o]ne who expects to be a party [] to an action that may be cognizable in a court of the state [] may obtain discovery [] for the purpose of perpetuating that person's own testimony or that of another natural person or organization, or of preserving evidence for use in the event an action is subsequently filed." Therefore, Respondent has standing to pursue her discovery requests. (part 1 of 3)
